topic: Shipping to 200 real companies: what breaks that unit tests never catch
format: A sprint retrospective: what shipped, what slipped, what surprised you — honest, specific, no PR spin
post:
We shipped to company #200 last week.

No champagne. We spent the day chasing a race condition that never showed up across 4,000 tests.

Here's the gap nobody talks about enough: tests passing and production holding are two different things.

Unit tests catch logic. They don't catch timing.

Our WPS payroll engine runs clean in isolation. But 12 companies hitting the same bank integration window simultaneously? One tenant's job bleeds into another's. Drizzle ORM, tRPC, MySQL — each one fine on its own. The seams between them are where real companies find the cracks.

Three things that only broke at scale:

Ministry of Labour compliance checks that fly through dev, then timeout under actual document volumes.

End-of-service gratuity calculations that were accurate for one employee, wrong for 40 when contract types mixed mid-batch.

Sanad office management workflows built around sequential requests — real offices don't work sequentially.

Honest retrospective: the architecture held. Our assumptions about usage patterns didn't.

The fix wasn't elegant. Queue depth limits and per-tenant job isolation. Boring. Correct.

I keep thinking about how many edge cases are still sitting quietly in the codebase, waiting for company #300 to find them. That's not doom — it's just the actual work. You don't know what you don't know until real load hits real users with real deadlines.

What's lurking in your stack that 200 real users will eventually surface?
